coredata.dc:title: Preparation and electrochemiluminescent and photoluminescent properties of a graphene oxide colloid
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yi
Huang, Guangming
Jiang, Jie
Zhang, Qing
Cui, Hua
coredata.dc:description:
A simple strategy was developed for the preparation of a graphene oxide (GO) colloid with good water-solubility and stability by the reaction of graphite oxide with nitric acid. The as-prepared GO colloid was characterized by atomic force microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and Raman spectroscopy. The results demonstrated that the GO colloid had an average height of 0.8nm, widths in the range ∼10 to 60nm, and contained abundant carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Strong electrochemiluminescent emission was observed from the GO colloid in the presence of K2S2O8. The electrochemiluminescent signal of this system was quite stable with time, and its mechanism was ascribed to the electron-transfer of GO colloid and
SO
4
· -
radicals produced by electroreduction of
S
2
O
8
2 -
ions. The photoluminescent activity of the GO colloid was also studied. The results indicated that it had a visible photoluminescence, excellent photostability, and stable photoluminescence intensity in a wide pH range.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GO colloid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The support of this research by the National Natural Science Foundation of PR China (Grant Nos. 21173201 , 21075115 , 20625517 and 20573101 ), the Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS (Grant No. SKLEAC201110 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant Nos. WK2060190007 ) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM image of GO colloid. (b) The cross section identified by the line shows the heights of GO colloid.
(a) High-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of GO colloid. (b) FTIR spectrum of GO colloid. (c) XRD patterns of graphite oxide and GO colloid. (d) Raman spectrum of GO colloid.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of a bare glassy carbon electrode in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4, containing 0.1M KCl) in the absence (dot line) and presence of 1mM K2S2O8 (solid line). Inset: the magnification of curves in the range from −1.4 to −0.5V. (b) ECL curves in the absence (dot line) and presence (solid line) of GO colloid with 1mM K2S2O8; inset: the corresponding cyclic voltammogram. The arrows indicate the scan direction. (c) ECL emission from the GO colloid under continuous cyclic voltammograms for 1000 cycles in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4, containing 0.1M KCl) in the presence of 1mM K2S2O8. (d) ECL curves of GO colloid in the presence of 1mM K2S2O8 with the potential cycled between 0 and −1.6V.
ECL spectrum of GO colloid with K2S2O8. The ECL spectrum was measured using high energy cutoff filters at wavelength of 450, 490, 550, 565, 580, 610 and 630nm.

ECL intensity of GO colloid in the absence and presence of Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, bovine serum albumin (BSA), hemoglobin (Hemin), lysine, and glucose (all at a concentration of 1mgmL−1), and fetal bovine serum.

(a) UV–vis absorption of GO colloid, inset: photographs of GO colloid aqueous solution taken under (upper) day light and (down) UV light (365nm, 18W). (b) PL (excited at 360nm) spectra of GO colloid. (c) PL spectra of GO colloid at different excitation wavelengths from 330 to 400nm. (d) Photostability test of GO colloid in a fluorescence spectrophotometer using 360nm excitation.
A illustration of the ECL mechanism for GO colloid with K2S2O8.

head.ce:title: Preparation and electrochemiluminescent and photoluminescent properties of a graphene oxide colloid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Soft Matter Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A simple strategy was developed for the preparation of a graphene oxide (GO) colloid with good water-solubility and stability by the reaction of graphite oxide with nitric acid. The as-prepared GO colloid was characterized by atomic force microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and Raman spectroscopy. The results demonstrated that the GO colloid had an average height of 0.8nm, widths in the range ∼10 to 60nm, and contained abundant carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Strong electrochemiluminescent emission was observed from the GO colloid in the presence of K2S2O8. The electrochemiluminescent signal of this system was quite stable with time, and its mechanism was ascribed to the electron-transfer of GO colloid and
SO
4
· -
radicals produced by electroreduction of
S
2
O
8
2 -
ions. The photoluminescent activity of the GO colloid was also studied. The results indicated that it had a visible photoluminescence, excellent photostability, and stable photoluminescence intensity in a wide pH range.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) has generated much excitement for various potential applications in energy, electronics, biosensing, drug delivery, and catalysis [1,2]. Nevertheless, the previous prepared GO is always extremely polydisperse and its size is typically microns or larger, and tend to induce aggregation in solution, which restricts their direct use in nanodevices and biologically related applications. One of the strategies for overcoming these problems above is to synthesize GO colloid, defined as a GO flake with nanometer-sized diameter [3–5], owing to its intrinsic photoluminescence (PL) properties, uniform size distribution, good water-solubility, and significantly improved stability in physiological solution. Dai’s group firstly synthesized GO colloid (<50nm) for drug delivery and cellular imaging [3,4]. Recently, a modified method for GO colloid synthesis using graphite nanofibers as the precursor is proposed by Huang et al. [5], which is more simple, fast and convenient. Nevertheless, it is difficult to obtain starting materials, graphite nanofibers, thus a simple and easy method for preparing GO colloid remains a challenge.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a kind of luminescence produced during electrochemical reactions in solutions, which has attracted considerate attention in both fundamental study and analytical applications owing to its remarkable advantages, such as simplicity, low cost, low background signal, and high sensitivity [6]. In recent years, owing to their unique optical and electrochemical properties, ECL of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) has been widely investigated [7–9]. Alternatively, compared with QDs, the GO colloid has lower toxicity and better biocompatibility. It is of great interest to investigate the ECL property of the GO colloid. Although the study about ECL emission of GO has been started by Bard’s group [10], involving in a significant amplification strategy of ECL intensity, stable ECL of the GO colloid in solution has not been investigated yet.

In previous studies, the ECL of functionalized GO by Ru(II) complex ([Ru-NH2]2+ and Ru(phen)3 2+) by covalent or noncovalent interaction was investigated [11,12]. The corresponding ECL emission originated from Ru(II) complex rather than GO. In this work, we report a novel, easy and simple strategy for the preparation of a GO colloid using graphite oxide as the precursor. The ECL performance of the prepared GO colloid was explored and strong ECL emission was observed from the GO colloid in the presence of K2S2O8 for the first time. The ECL intensity is quite stable with time against multiple scan cycles (0 to −1.4V), metal ions, small molecules, biomacromolecules, and physiological solutions including serum. The obtained GO colloid showed good water-solubility. The intrinsic photoluminescent property of the GO colloid was also studied and a distinct yellowish green PL was observed.

Graphite, HNO3, KCl, K2S2O8, d-glucose, l-lysine, CoCl2, CuSO4 and Pb(NO3)2 were purchased from Shanghai reagent (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin, and hemoglobin were obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). The dialysis tube (molecular weight cutoff, MWCO: 3000) was obtained from JingKeHongDa Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Fetal bovine serum was purchased from Guangzhou Ruite Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). All reagents are of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Graphite oxide was prepared by oxidation of graphite according to a modified Hummers method [13]. 50mL of H2SO4 was added into the 250mL flask filled with 2.0g graphite at room temperature, followed by addition of 7.0g KMnO4 slowly at 0°C (ice bath). After increase temperature to 35°C, the mixture was stirred by magnetic stirring bar for 2h. Excess water was added into the mixture at 0°C (ice bath) and then H2O2 (30wt.%) was added until there was no gas producing. Filtration with glass filter afforded brown powder (graphite oxide) and the powder was dried at room temperature.

The GO colloid was prepared by nitric acid treatment of graphite oxide. In a typical synthesis, 0.2g graphite oxide was placed in a 100-mL three-neck flask and then 50mL of 5molL−1 HNO3 was added, followed by refluxing for 2days. After cooling to the room temperature, the suspension was centrifuged (12,500rmin−1) for 45min to remove unreacted graphite oxide. The supernatant was neutralized and further dialyzed for 48h against ultra-pure water (18.2MΩ) through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3kDa).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were taken using a DI Innova scanning probe microscope. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent 8453). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALABMKII electron spectrograph with Mg Kα radiation as the X-ray source. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained from a Japan Rigaku D/max X-ray diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with a VECTOR22 FTIR spectrometer. PL spectra were measured on a model F-7000 spectrofluorophotometer. Raman spectrum was recorded with a LabRamHR equipped by an Ar+ laser giving the excitation line of 514.5nm.

ECL measurements were performed with a homemade ECL/electrochemical cell system, including a model CHI760B electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China), an H-type electrochemical cell (self-designed), a model CR-105 photomultiplier tube (PMT) (Beijing, China), a model RFL-1 luminometer (Xi′an, China) and a computer, as described previously [14]. A three-electrode system composed of glassy carbon electrode (3-mm-diameter) as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and a silver wire as the quasi-reference electrode (AgQRE). Although the potential of the AgQRE was essentially stable during an experiment, the measurements of ΔE = E Ag/Ag+ − E SCE in different solutions were taken for potential calibrations. A K2S2O8 solution containing 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and 0.1M KCl (pH 7.4) was used as working solution. During measurements, a 2.0mL portion of the working solution was added to the working compartment, followed by addition of 200μL GO colloid solution, and the blank solution without K2S2O8 was added to the auxiliary compartment of the ECL cell, respectively.

ECL emissions were produced with cyclic voltammograms at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 and recorded. High voltage applied to the PMT was maintained at −900V. The ECL spectrum was measured using high energy cutoff filters at wavelength of 450, 490, 550, 565, 580, 610 and 630nm. ΔI fλ was calculated as shown: e.g., ΔI f450 = I blank (without filter)− I 450, ΔI f490 = I 450 − I 490, etc. [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GO colloid
ce:section.ce:para: The GO colloid in this study was synthesized from the graphite oxide by refluxing with nitric acid for two days, and further dialyzed for 48h against ultra-pure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3kDa). The as-prepared GO colloid was found to be ∼10 to 60nm (Fig. 1 a) in lateral width (characterized by AFM) and its average height was 0.8nm (Fig. 1b). The thickness of single layer GO was firstly determined by Ruoff and coworkers [16]. The obtained result demonstrated that the thickness of individual GO sheets was approximate 1nm. Thus most of prepared GO colloid was single layered. Moreover, we attempted to employ transmission electron microscope (TEM) or scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to get TEM or SEM images, but the corresponding images were not obtained, which may be related to the bad electroconductivity of GO colloid because it contained many oxygen groups.

XPS measurements were carried out to determine the composition of the GO colloid. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2 a) shows three peaks at 284.6, 286.4, 288.7eV, which can be attributed to CC, C–O, and COOH [17,18]. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2b) displays the characteristic absorption bands of carboxyl group at 3435 and 3250cm−1 O–H stretching vibration, 1721cm−1 CO stretching vibration, and 1424cm−1 C–O (COO−) vibration. The peaks around 1620, and 1385cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of CC, and N–O (which originated from the residue of
NO
3
-
) bonds [19,20], respectively. Meanwhile, the existence of hydroxyl groups is demonstrated, through the 1244cm−1 C–OH vibration [21,22]. The observations are consistent with the results of XPS. All these results confirmed that the GO colloid contained abundant carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups.

We also characterized the crystal structure of the GO colloid by XRD. The GO colloid did not show any diffraction in the 2θ ≈10° region characteristic of graphite oxide (Fig. 2c), indicating that the complete exfoliation of the graphite oxide [23,24]. Raman spectrum of the GO colloid exhibit a D band at 1385cm−1 arising from a breathing mode of k-point phonons of A1g symmetry and a G band at 1605cm−1 (Fig. 2d) corresponding to the first-order scattering of the E 2g vibration mode. The strong background signal may be related to the PL of the GO colloid. The stability of the GO colloid was also investigated, the as-prepared GO colloid solution was found to long-term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation at 4°C and natural environment, acid or weak base conditions for at least 3months (Fig. S1, Supplementary information) and 2months, respectively.

The ECL of the GO colloid dispersed in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4, containing 0.1M KCl) in the presence of K2S2O8 was studied on a glassy carbon working electrode. Strong ECL emission (Fig. 3 b) was observed in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4, containing 0.1M KCl) in the presence of 1mM K2S2O8, a coreactant commonly used in ECL systems, when the potential was cycled between +1.6V and −1.6V. Fig. 3a shows the cyclic voltammograms of a bare glassy carbon (GC) electrode in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4, containing 0.1M KCl) with or without 1mM K2S2O8. The cathodic reduction peak at about −1.0V (insert of Fig. 3a) corresponds to the reduction of
S
2
O
8
2 -
ions on GC electrode [25]. After mixing K2S2O8 with the GO colloid solution, the mixture solution exhibited a strong ECL emission peak at −1.14V, which was about 10 times higher than that of K2S2O8 (Fig. 3b).

This red-shift was also observed in the π-conjugated organic molecules including perylene, terrylene, quaterrylene diimides, and alkynylpyrene derivatives and other organic molecules such as star-shaped D-A compounds with a 1,3,5-triazine core and substituted fluorene arms, (dppy)BTPA, and BODIPY dyes. The red-shift of the ECL spectra was explained by self-absorption at the shorter wavelengths (an inner-filter effect) in the ECL solution, which was more concentrated than the solutions used for PL measurements, and a small instrumental offset [26–29]. GO has a large π-conjugated structure [30,31], thus the red-shift of the ECL spectrum of GO colloid relative to the PL spectrum normally might be attributed to an inner filter effect owing to the high sample concentration used for ECL and also to a small instrumental offset. All the above results demonstrated that the observed ECL emission was originated from the GO colloid.

From Fig. 3c, when consecutive scans from 0 to −1.4V at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 were performed for more than 1000 cycles, no obvious change was observed. That indicates the ECL intensity of the GO colloid –
S
2
O
8
2 -
system is highly repeatable over time. The effect of electrode materials including gold, indium tin oxide, and glassy carbon on the ECL behaviors was also studied (Fig. S2, Supplementary information), the ECL intensity of GC electrode was equal to that of gold and indium tin oxide electrodes, revealing that electrode materials did not influence the ECL process. Moreover, the ECL system of the GO colloid –
S
2
O
8
2 -
could not be quenched by metal ions (Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+), small molecules (d-glucose, l-lysine, adenosine), biomacromolecules (bovine serum albumin and hemoglobin) and physiological solutions including fetal bovine serum (Fig. 5). The results implied that this ECL system had excellent stability and anti-interference capability, suggesting attractive applications of the GO colloid in ECL sensing.

The ECL mechanism of the GO colloid was also investigated in this work. It was found that the ECL signal of the GO colloid in the absence of K2S2O8 could not be detected (Fig. S3, Supplementary information), implying that
S
2
O
8
2 -
played an important role in the ECL process as a coreactant. Besides, the cathodic ECL of the GO colloid was also investigated as the potential was cycled between 0 and −1.6V (Fig. 3d). Compared with the ECL between +1.6 and −1.6V (Fig. 3b), the intensity of cathodic ECL did not show significant change, indicating that the ECL emission was dominantly contributed by coreactant pathway. Above observation implies the ECL behavior of the GO colloid might share the mechanism similar to ECL of Au nanoclusters and semiconductor quantum dots [32,33]. Therefore, the ECL mechanism of the GO colloid might involve in the formation of excited-state GO colloid (GO∗) by electron-transfer of negatively charged GO - colloid and the strongly oxidizing
SO
4
· -
radicals produced by electroreduction of
S
2
O
8
2 -
ions and the decay of GO∗ to GO colloid as shown in Scheme 1 .

The other optical properties of the GO colloid have also been investigated. No significant UV–Vis absorption was found (Fig. 6 a), while strong yellowish green PL emission was observed under irradiation of a 365nm lamp (18W) (Fig. 6a, inset). When the GO colloid was excited at 360nm, PL emission spectrum of the GO colloid was found to be centered at 500nm (Fig. 6b). Unlike other luminescent carbon nanomaterials (carbon dots and graphene quantum dots) with excitation-dependent PL behavior [34,35], when the excitation wavelength was between 330 and 400nm, the emission wavelength of our GO colloid remained constant (Fig. 6c).

According to previous study [2,36], the PL mechanisms of the GO colloid could be attributed to effects of size, surface state and oxygen-containing functional groups. However, the exact mechanisms responsible for the PL from the GO colloid in this study remain to be elucidated. The photostability of the GO colloid was also studied by monitoring the change of the PL intensity with irradiation time using the Xe light (150W). After irradiation for 13h, no distinct photobleaching was observed (Fig. 6d), demonstrating that the GO colloid was photostable. The effect of pH on the PL property of the GO colloid was investigated as shown in Fig. S4 (Supplementary information). The integrated PL intensity did not change significantly in the pH range of 3–11, indicating the potential application of our GO colloid in a wide range of pH.

A GO colloid has been synthesized by a simple and easy method by refluxing graphite oxide with nitric acid. The as-prepared GO colloid was found to be about 10–60nm in width and 0.8nm in average thickness, indicating that most of the GO colloid was single layered. ECL of the GO colloid dispersed in 0.1M PBS in the presence of K2S2O8 was observed, which might possibly be ascribed to electron-transfer of negatively charged GO - colloid and the strongly oxidizing
SO
4
· -
radicals. The GO colloid also possess excellent PL property as well as water-solubility and stability. These unique properties of the GO colloid are envisioned to have promising applications in bioimaging, drug carriers, optical devices, and bioassays.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of this research by the National Natural Science Foundation of PR China (Grant Nos. 21173201, 21075115, 20625517 and 20573101), the Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS (Grant No. SKLEAC201110), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant Nos. WK2060190007) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.01.024.
